condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

comment.narrative: Government monitoring of social protection reforms is manual and does not cover all areas of reform performance
The dashboard is finalized and populated with sex and age disaggregated data, including M&E Framework data
The dashboard is to inaguarted by the govt and populated with sex and age disaggregated data, including M&E Framework data
Functioning dashboard with M&E sex and age disaggregated data developed for CMC sub-committee / M&E committee review, follow-up, and feedback to line ministries on social protection reform progress

Dashboard with M&E functioning developed for CMC sub-committee /M&E committee review, follow-up, and feedback to line ministries on social protection reform progress with ministries expected to share their sex and age disaggregated data.

title.narrative: Strengthened key line ministries (Cabinet Division, GED, MoSW, MoF, MoLE) capacity for gender-responsive SP decision making

description.narrative: Strengthened key line ministries (Cabinet Division, GED, MoSW, MoF, MoLE) capacity for gender-responsive SP decision making

comment.narrative: Gender Strategy and Action Plan exists; however, line ministries have a limited understanding of how to implement gender-responsive SP reforms
Draft gender-responsive SSP policy for harmonized, effective, and efficient impact developed by key line ministries and available for stakeholder feedback

The SSPS Programme provided orientaion to govt and collaborated with the UNICEF TA Project Support to CMC and organized and attended seven sub-national level consultative dialogues, where orientation on NSSS and its Action Plan was one of the important components.
SSP policy harmonized and included gender-focused programs for children and women for effective and efficient impact and incorporated into NSSS 2026+

The SPPS team actively engaged with the interim government's Gender Reform Task Team to address policy gaps and bottlenecks affecting women's empowerment. By contributing evidence and policy recommendations, including from its 2020 Gender Strategy and Action Plan, the taskforce agreed to adopt key recommendations from the strategy and action plan. Previously, a high-level dialogue reached into consensus on gender-responsive social protection reforms and incorporation into NSSS 2026+.

condition.narrative: Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

(M&E plan) 6: Number of new groups or initiatives created through USG funding dedicated to resolving the conflict or the drivers of conflict (M&E plan) 7: Number of laws, policies, regulation or standards Addressing sustainable landscapes formally proposed, adapted or implemented as supported by USG assistance(M&E plan). 8: Number of hectares (seedlings) in all other areas under improved NRM, safeguards or sustainable practices (M&E plan) 9: Number of people trained in sustainable landscapes supported by USG assistance (M&E plan). 10: Number of people trained in the project management and finance related issues supported by USG assistance (Project) 11: Number of people reached by a USG funded intervention providing GBV services(M&E plan). 12: Percentage of female participants in USG-assisted programs designed to increase access to productive economic resources (assets, credit, income or employment).

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Number of houses made resilient against climate disasters (cyclones and floods)

description.narrative: This output will assist the most vulnerable households in the char communities of Lakshmitari and Mujibnagar to retrofit their houses against these climate change impacts.

title.narrative: Number of nano-grids installed and operational

description.narrative: The activities under this output will, therefore, increase electricity access in these communities by implementing decentralised nano-grids which are robust against floods and cyclones.

title.narrative: Number of household rainwater harvesting systems installed and operational

description.narrative: Under this output, rainwater harvesting systems will be installed, with all cyclone and flood resistant features, in Lakshmitari and Mujibnagar

title.narrative: Number of water user groups established and trained.

description.narrative: Community members will be supported to establish water-user groups. These groups will be trained to: i) manage the preservation and distribution of surface water in areas that experience water stress during the dry season and/or because of saline intrusion; and ii) assist community members with the maintenance of rainwater harvesting systems

title.narrative: Number of dual-purpose cluster house/ disaster shelters constructed and in use

description.narrative: 20 dual-purpose cluster house/ disaster shelters constructed and in use and beneficiaries will be women-led households.

title.narrative: Km of damaged embankments repaired/ strengthened.

description.narrative: Degraded embankments will be strengthened with a combination of geotextiles and the planting of vetiver grass and mangrove trees where appropriate.

title.narrative: Km of riverbank strengthened

description.narrative: Vulnerable riverbank will be strengthened with a combination of geotextiles and vetiver grass. Embankments will be strengthened according to the established best practices in Bangladesh.

title.narrative: Number of community embankment management groups established

description.narrative: The maintenance of embankments will be improved by increasing the involvement of local communities. Community embankment management groups (also known as water management organisations [WMOs]) will be mobilised for participation in the repair of embankments and the operation and maintenance of embankments.

title.narrative: Km2 of char areas mapped

description.narrative: Fine-scale climate hazard maps will be produced for ten selected chars. The hazard maps will be based on existing data from various sources. The resulting maps will focus in particular on flooding, storm damage, and river erosion, as well as socio-economic characteristics, infrastructure, basic services and natural ecosystems that buffer against climate disasters.

title.narrative: Number of households in Mujibnagar receiving periodic updates during cyclone risk periods

description.narrative: The activities will consequently modernise and expand the CPP in this area to provide timely early warning of cyclones and adequate on-site responses at the necessary scale. This will include tailoring early warnings and cyclone preparedness to local requirements and using the local language.

title.narrative: Number of CPP volunteers trained

description.narrative: The training programmes will include search and rescue, water rescue, first aid and the use of light rescue equipment, and will incorporate gender, psycho-social and disability considerations.

title.narrative: Existing cyclone shelters in Mujibnagar provisioned with CPP equipment

description.narrative: Existing cyclone shelters and the cluster houses constructed by this project will be provided with full sets of modern cyclone preparedness equipment.

title.narrative: Number of field school sessions held;

description.narrative: The farmer field schools will function as hubs for research on climate-resilient agriculture and will host visits for farmers from other chars and for agricultural researchers from universities and research centres in Bangladesh.

title.narrative: Number of people trained in climate-resilient agricultural practices

description.narrative: 7,500 farmers will be trained on climate-resilient agricultural practices and proven innovative agricultural practices.

title.narrative: Number of cold storage units installed and operational

description.narrative: Two cold storage facilities will be established in Mujibnagar, as well as in Lakshmitari. These facilities will reduce the wastage of harvested crops and fish and will increase food security at a local level during climate-induced disaster events.

title.narrative: Hectares of agricultural land irrigated

description.narrative: The irrigation systems will draw water from existing tube wells in Lakshmitari and will be powered by independent PV systems or by the nano-grids installed.

title.narrative: Number of people provided with technology, skills and materials to make their livelihoods climate resilient.

description.narrative: This support will benefit the most vulnerable inhabitants of the chars by assisting them to gain skills and access income generation opportunities, thereby improving their overall adaptive capacity.

title.narrative: Number of staff from local government institutions, Bangladesh Water Board and Department of Agriculture trained.

description.narrative: local government representatives and staff will be trained on the relevant ecosystem-based and community-based adaptation measures so that they can facilitate the uptake of these measures among char communities.

title.narrative: Number of adaptation innovation centres established

description.narrative: Two innovation and knowledge centres will be established in each target area (two in Mujibnagar and two in Lakshmitari) to collect local best practices and adaptation innovations, and to disseminate this knowledge across each target area.

title.narrative: Number of people reached by awareness-raising campaigns

description.narrative: Teachers and religious leaders will be trained to disseminate climate change information to schoolchildren and other community members through schools and community awareness programmes in Mujibnagar, Lakshmitari and the surrounding areas.

title.narrative: Number of knowledge products developed

description.narrative: Many of the knowledge products will be generated through component 1 of the project which will produce training modules, develop applications and databases and establish platforms for both the private and public sector on climate change adaptation.

title.narrative: Social and Environmental Screening (SES)

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Ecosystem based management of

description.narrative: The project objective is to apply an ecosystem-based framework for managing Ecologically Critical Areas (ECAs) in Bangladesh to enhance the conservation of globally significant biodiversity and support local livelihoods. It is aimed at addressing the increased degradation of wetland habitats from unsustainable development and local community practices that is leading to biodiversity loss.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1: Area of new wetland declared as Environmentally Critical Area under Bangladesh Environment Conservation Act of 1995 (revised 2010) 2: Number of direct fisher and agricultural beneficiaries with increased incomes (15% increase) disaggregated by gender 3: Gender-responsive measures in place for conservation, sustainable use, and equitable access to and benefit sharing of natural resources, biodiversity and ecosystems as indicated by: a) Policy/planning frameworks, b) Legal and regulatory frameworks, c) Institutional frameworks 4: Level of institutional capacities for integrated ecosystem-based planning, management and monitoring of ECA as measured by UNDP’s capacity development scorecard 5: Percentage of ECAs with defined ecosystem-based framework/system and standards to achieve favourable ecological conditions 6: Implementation of 2016 ECA rules and its compliance as measured/indicated by: a) Sustainable financing strategy and action plan, b) Ecology management fund 7: Number of ECA co-management plans adequately sourced in terms of staff and resources and approved by government 8: Improvement in water quality in ECAs in terms of pH, DO, COD, BOD, N and P and other relevant parameters as determined by ECA frameworks 9: a) Number of Village Conservation Groups (VCGs) formed, b) Total Number of VCG members with at least 30% female representation 10: Establishment and technical capacity of ECA Management, Monitoring & Compliance Unit (MMCU) within DoE as measured by MMCU established and operational 11: Increase in level of knowledge (disaggregated by gender) on ecosystem-based approaches as defined by the following: a) Number of community members trained and adopting new technologies and practices, b) Communication strategy and action plan developed and effectively implemented 12: Number of knowledge products that reflects best practices and lessons learned

comment.narrative: 1: 10,825 ha of Halda River (2019) 2: 750 fishers and 100 agricultural farmers (2019) 3: Gender based policies and practices not adequately addressed due to lack of awareness, capacity and commitment (2019) 4: Limited institutional capacities for planning, management and monitoring of ecosystem-based approaches to ECAs as measured by UNDP Capacity Development Scorecard baseline values of 29 (2019) 5: 0% (2019) 6: Currently, 2016 ECA Management Rules have yet to be applied and there is no scientific framework against which compliance monitoring can be applied (2019) 7: 0 (2019) 8: DO (0.93-5.15 mg/L); BOD5 (30-545 mg/L); COD (43-983 mg/L); pH (6.3-7.3); EC (110-524 uS/cm); Chloride (12-56 mg/L); Alkalinity (35-67 mg/L); and Hardness (38-121 mg/L) (2019) 9: a) 0, b) 0 (2019) 10: Limited capacity and technical competence in wetland restoration and management prevent DoE from fulfilling its mandate in safeguarding ECAs, all of which are wetlands (2019) 11: TBD (2019) 12: Less than 5 (2019)
N/A (Project implementation has not started in 2020)

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1: NAP formulated and communicated based on identified CCA priorities and in close coordination with plans already in place 2: Standards and a protocol established to track CCA project financing and investments

comment.narrative: 1: NAP process begun with NAP roadmap, but NAP document has not been drafted and implementation workplan and communications strategy are not in place. (2018) 2: A climate fiscal framework was established in 2014 but is no longer operational. Standards and protocols with comprehensive functional tracking mechanisms for climate finance do not exist. (2018)
1: NAP preparation process initiated 2: A functional adaptation finance tracking mechanism is conceptualized

1: NAP process begun with NAP roadmap, but NAP document has not been drafted and implementation workplan and communications strategy are not in place. 2: A climate fiscal framework was established in 2014 but is no longer operational. Standards and protocols with comprehensive functional tracking mechanisms for climate finance do not exist.
1. Developed NAP zero draft 2. Structure and architecture of CCIKM web-portal developed
1. NAP zero draft developed but not yet validated and reviewed 2. Structure and architecture of CCIKM web-portal developed but not yet operational

1. Synthesis report on climate risk and vulnerability, gap analysis report of climate change mainstreaming in planning and budgeting systems across government and capacity needs assessment report to steer the NAP process at the national level, proposed institutional framework and coordination mechanism, national NAP mandate drafted, CCIKM portal, climate vulnerability scenarios available for several prioritized sector, synopsis on valuation of economic impacts 2. Final Draft NAP document 3.Draft capacity building action plan on CC, integration of CCA in 5-year development plan, developed climate risk screening tools, developed racking indicators for IMWG and at NDA, MoEFCC 4. Adaptation finance tracking mechanism incorporated into the CCIKM portal, costed NAP document, analysis of CCA financing options

1. Developed institutional coordination and climate change (CC) information and knowledge management (CCIKM) for medium- to long-term planning. 2. Adaptation options appraised and prioritized, and the National Adaptation Plan formulated including peer and public review processes, implementation work plan and M&E plan in place, and communications strategy and outreach plan in place. 3. Climate risk-informed decision-making tools developed to piloted by planning and budget departments at national and sectoral levels. 4. Adaptation finance tracking mechanism adopted, CCA actions within NAP costed, comprehensive analysis of options for CCA financing conducted and financing sources public and private –international and domestic options identified for priority sectors and in priority regions of the country.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Low Carbon Urban Development

description.narrative: The project Promoting Energy-Related Low Carbon Urban Development in Bangladesh (LCUD) addresses the development challenge of increased negative economic and environmental impacts from fossil use and other non-sustainable practices in urban areas by enabling low-carbon interventions and investments in energy-efficiency (EE) and renewable energy (RE) in buildings and built environment and to support urban development in Bangladesh.

title.narrative: Accelerator Lab – Bangladesh

description.narrative: Increased UNDP capability for scanning and sensemaking. Identify and Elevate New Solutions. Global Learning and Scaling Network

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative: 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level. 1.2 Diversity of different data sources used 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented 1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP) 2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners 2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite 2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies 3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network. 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network. 3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.

comment.narrative: 1.1 - 2 new sources of data per year 1.2 - 3 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 6 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 2 innovation methods per Action Plan 2.1 - 1 blogs, or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least one government entity or partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods. 2.3 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year. 3.2 - 5% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Network´s work is featured at least once per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Identify and engage with 2 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

The lab was established at the end of 2020 with the last member joining on 01 November. This did not allow much time for the lab to work towards the targets set for Year 1. Based on the 100 Days Action Plan and activities, the lab was able to achieve the following results:
1.1 - 3 new sources of data were introduced (Digital Data for Development database of World Bank Group and LinkedIn, ADB’s Real-Time Assessment Using Bangladesh and Sri Lanka Online Job Portals, Social Media Engagement Data for AccLab’s Violence Against Women video) 1.2 - 3 different type of data sources (LinkedIn Data, BDJobs Job-Portal Data, and Social Media Engagement Data) 1.3 - The lab was able to identify 6 new potential solutions during 3 months in 2020 (VAW Video, One-Stop Service Centre for Women Entrepreneurs in Host Community, Youth Employment Accelerator Program Initiative – Bangladesh, Ethnic Community Based Tourism in Cox’s Bazaar, Mutual Fund for LoGIC, Employment in Dry Fish Industry) 1.4 - 5 innovation methods were used for the 2 Action Plans (Foresight, Future Analysis, Sensemaking, Positive Deviance, Horizon Scanning)
2.1 - 1 media post published (‘Ami Shei Kal Joyi Nari’ video on Violence Against Women) 2.2 - One government entity/partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods (Prime Minister’s Office) 2.3 - 100% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects (Mutual Fund for LoGIC project, Grant Facility for SWM project, Blue Economy for CCP project). 3.1 - The lab has interacted at least once a week with another lab in the network. Majority of interactions were with labs in South Asia and other labs across the network working on similar frontier challenges as Bangladesh. 3.2 - At least 5% of information shared by Bangladesh AccLab across the network were on methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Network´s work was featured at least once per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team had engaged at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. 3.4 - The lab connected its publications to local development ecosystem through social media (Prothom Alo and CPD). It also identified and engaged over 5 new relevant local social media profiles (new artists on-boarded for VAW video).

1.1 - 5 new data sources per year 1.2 - 6 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 35 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 5 innovation methods per Action Plan. 2.1 - At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 - At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method. 2.4 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year. 3.2 - 25% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Networks work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

1.1 - 14 new sources of data were introduced
1. edX Platform Data of Bangladeshi and Indian Users (made available by edX to Lab) 2. Data of SSC (Secondary School Certificate) exam candidates learning behavior collected through a custom Learning Management System (LMS) deployed by the Lab 3. Data of social media users on their engagement (Facebook) on Labs ‘Ami Shei Kal Joyi Nari’ video 4. Data of Womens Digital Innovation Hackathon participants learning through post-hack survey 5. Data of disaster and climate change expenditure of 3095 rural households from 10 selected districts 6. Data of Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) employer skills demand and skills gaps (Study completed with 50 companies surveyed) 7. Data of ICT/CSE students on their perspective on micro-credentials (Study completed with 1000+ students surveyed) 8. Data of host community youths employment status and their ICT readiness. 9. Data of community-based tourism on informal employment (profile and evaluation data of beach workers) 10. Data on informal worker - tour guides in Coxs Bazar (training and evaluation data of tour guides) 11. Data on informal employment - boat operators in Bandarban (profile and evaluation data of boat operators) 12. Data on informal employment - Jeep drives in Bandarban (training and evaluation data) 13. Data on unemployed rural women in host community (socio-economic, poverty, income and demographic data) 14. Multi-dimensional poverty index data - specialized in social safety-net data and World Bank Group socio-economic data)
1.2 - 8 different type of data sources (MOOC usage data, LMS data, social media data, Citizen generated data, primary survey data, secondary survey data, GIS data, interviews)
1.3 - 39 new potential solutions identified and documented in the Solutions Platform.
1.4 - 12 innovation methods were used for the 2 Action Plans 1. Informality Patters in Bangladesh - Behaviorial Insights, Data Visualization, System Thinking, Participatory Needs Assessment. 2. Coastal Belt IGA - Behavioral Insights, Collective Intelligence, Micro Narratives, Randomized Controlled Trials, System Thinking. 3. YEAPI-B - Co-creation, Future Analysis, Hackathons, Horizon Scanning, Sensemaking.
2.1 - 7 blogs published 1. Generating informal employment opportunities for local residents in tourism: The case of Tangail district of Bangladesh 2. Accelerating Equitable E-Learning in Bangladesh 3. Financing disaster-induced expenses: findings from a Household Expenditure Survey in Bangladesh 4. Women’s Digital Innovation Hackathon 5. Learning from Exploration: Entrepreneurship instilling changemaking in the future minds of Bangladesh 6. Thanchi: Rising star of Community-based Tourism 7. Through our lens:Community-based Tourism unlocking the potentials of informal employment opportunities in Thanchi (Bangladesh)
2.2 - 100% learning challenges have the public sector or other partners as counterpart 1. Informality Patterns in Bangladesh - Bangladesh Tourism Board, Local Government, ICT Division. 2. Coastal Belt IGA Opportunities for Poverty Reduction in Bangladesh - GED, Womens Chamber of Commerce, Tour Operators Association, Tourist Police, Coxs Bazar Chamber of Commerce, Jahangirnagar University, Dhaka University. 3. e-Learning Pilot Project - Volunteers Associaton for Bangladresh 4. Whole of Society approach in Tourism - Bangladesh Tourism Board, CHT Local Government. 5. YEAPI-B - edX, Reaktor, Women in Digital, Univeristy of Helsinki, UNLEASH Innovation Lab
2.3 - Three government entity/partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods 1. Bangladesh Tourism Board 2. Tourist Police 3. Chittagong Hill Tracts Local Government
2.4 - 83% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects 1. Mutual Fund support for LoGIC project 2. Grant Facility support for SWM project 3. Toursim & Informality support for SID CHT Project 4. Blue Economy support for CCP project 5. NICE platform experiment support for a2i project.
3.1 - 100% achieved (48+). The lab has interacted at least once a week with another lab in the network. Majority of interactions were with labs in Asia and other labs across the network working on similar challenges and innovation methods as Bangladesh.
3.2 - 100% achieved. At least 25% of information shared by Bangladesh AccLab across the network were on methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related (UNDP Womens Digital Innovation Hackathon, YEAPI-B & edX collaboration).
3.3 - Approximately 50% acheived. The Accelerator Lab Network´s work was featured periodically in the Country Office Social Media channels and tagged UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts over the year 2021.
Approximately 50% acheived. The Lab team had engaged periodically across social media conversations related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account over the year 2021.
3.4 - The lab connected its publications to local development ecosystem through social media for several interventions (Blogs, Hackathon, Ami Shei Kal Joyi Nari video, Behaviorial Insights, ICT Literacy, First-aid, Tour training, Photography, Safe drive, Prothom Alo and CPD).
The Lab engaged 13 new relevant local and individual social media profiles (new artists on-boarded for Ami Shei Kal Joyi Nari video included singers and artists - Amzad Hossain, Elita Karim, Palbasha Siddique, Armeen Musa, Tashfee, Nahar, Johan Alamgir, D-rockstar Shuvo, Labik Kamal, Moktadir Dewan, Sayon Mansang, MG Kibria), and 3 institutional profiles - Women in Digital (@WID_Network) and UNLEASH (@UNLEASHlab).

1.1 - 5 new data sources per year 1.2 - 6 different type of data sources per year 1.3 - 35 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 - 5 innovation methods per Action Plan. 2.1 - At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 - At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 - At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method. 2.4 - At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. 3.1 - The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year. 3.2 - 25% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 - The Accelerator Lab Networks work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. 3.4 - Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

1.1 - 8 new sources of data were introduced 1.2 - 7 different type of data sources 1.3 - 42 new potential solutions identified and documented in the Solutions Platform. 1.4 - 6 innovation methods were used
2.1 - 7 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published 2.2 - 100% learning challenges have the public sector or other partners as counterpart 2.3 - Three government entity/partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods 1. Bangladesh Tourism Board 2. Tourist Police 3. Chittagong Hill Tracts Local Government
2.4 - 83% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects 1. Mutual Fund support for LoGIC project 2. Grant Facility support for SWM project 3. Toursim & Informality support for SID CHT Project 4. Blue Economy support for CCP project 5. NICE platform experiment support for a2i project.
3.1 - 100% achieved (48+). The lab has interacted at least once a week with another lab in the network. Majority of interactions were with labs in Asia and other labs across the network working on similar challenges and innovation methods as Bangladesh.
3.2 - 100% achieved. At least 25% of information shared by Bangladesh AccLab across the network were on methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related
3.3 - 100% achieved. The Accelerator Lab Network´s work was featured at least 3 times in the Country Office Social Media channels and tagged UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts.
The Lab team had engaged periodically (more than once per month) across social media conversations related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account
3.4 - 100% achieved. The lab connected its publications to the local development ecosystem by engaging over 5 relevant local social media profiles across several interventions.

title.narrative: IP- Transformative Governance for Sustainable Development

description.narrative: Aligning with the priorities of the government and UNDP relating to democratic governance, the IP aims to strengthen key public institutions. The IP consists of seven components/ outputs as follows:• Supporting the Bangladesh Parliament and MPs to evidence-based policy formulation • Engaging with the Governance Innovation Unit (GIU to advance governance innovation solutions) • Promoting civic education for responsive citizenship to support sustainable LDC graduation • Enabling peaceful national election, strengthening citizen engagement and policy support • Supporting access to affordable justice and legal aid services • Proving technical assistance to NGOAB and IMED to improve monitoring and oversight functions of development projects• Promoting transformative economic policies for sustainable, inclusive, and green economic growth, trade and investment in Bangladesh

title.narrative: Partnerships for a More Tolerant, Inclusive Bangladesh (PTIB), Phase II

description.narrative: Partnerships for a Tolerant, Inclusive Bangladesh (PTIB) is a multi-year initiative to understand and prevent hate speech, violence, and extremism in Bangladesh. This second phase of the programme (2023-2024) builds on the foundations of the project’s first phase and no-cost extension (2017-2023) that supported Bangladesh’s efforts to understand sources of violence, engage citizens as messengers of positive messages of tolerance and inclusivity, and develop a coherent and contextual strategy to prevent violent extremism (PVE).

title.narrative: Social Protection Policy Support Programme, Phase II

description.narrative: The Social Protection Policy Support (SPPS) Programme is a flagship programme of the Cabinet Division and General Economics Division of Bangladesh Planning Commission for social security reforms in Bangladesh. The Programme acts as a catalyst for enrolling the National Social Security Strategy (NSSS) and its guided reforms, by ensuring the broadest stakeholder participation, expert advice, and capacity development. The project proposal addresses reform gaps: National Social Security Strategy (NSSS) action plans and their short and medium-term implementation. The Programme commenced in 2014 and is being implemented with the support of the Government of Bangladesh (GoB), DFAT, FCDO (formerly known as DFID), and UNDP.

title.narrative: Annual Output Results

Dedicated NSSS Central Management Committee (CMC)/Sub-Committee/M&E Committee headed by Cabinet Secretary/Secretary C&R, Cabinet Division/Member (Secretary) GED activated with fit for purpose composition and scope for efficient implementation of reforms plans.

description.narrative: SPPS Project will provide support to the NSSS Central Management Committee (CMC)/Sub-Committee/M&E Committee headed by Cabinet Secretary/Secretary C&R, Cabinet Division/Member (Secretary) GED activated with fit for purpose composition and scope for efficient implementation of reforms plans.

comment.narrative: The government is in agreement to activate the committee with support from the project
Oversee the implementation of the reform action plans as in the APA by commissioning the 2nd MTR

Implementation of the reform action plans as in the APA by commissioning the 2nd MTR is supported by the dedicated NSSS Central Management Committee (CMC)/Sub-Committee/M&E Committee headed by Cabinet Secretary/Secretary C&R, Cabinet Division/Member (Secretary), GED.
Oversee the implementation of the reform action plans as in the APA Decisions on 2nd MTR findings and recommendation

The CMC oversaw consultations with 39 ministries and five thematic clusters as part of the NSSS Action Plan monitoring exercise. CMC members reviewed a draft report, with key findings to be presented at the Bangladesh Social Security Conference 2025. The APA Committee in the Cabinet Division evaluated ministries' performance and provided guidance to ensure accurate reflection of NSSS activities in the APA. Additionally, advisory support was given during the CODI workshop to aid EU reporting and budget support, while the LCG Governance and Social Protection meeting strengthened engagement between the Government of Bangladesh (GoB) and development partners (DPs) for improved social protection accountability. These actions reflect the activation and fit-for-purpose composition of the CMC for effective reform monitoring and evaluation.
The final NSSS Evaluation shows significant reform progress

In 2025, the NSSS CMC/M&E mechanism, chaired by the Cabinet Secretary, steered the final NSSS evaluation and translation of findings into concrete reforms. A reflective review with 39 ministries and five thematic clusters informed CMC decisions to consolidate programmes from 140 to 95, expand Old Age Allowance and disability coverage, and scale up G2P payments to 40.05% of all social protection transfers. The National Conference on Social Protection 2025, based on the evaluation and research papers, confirmed significant progress in lifecycle-based coverage, targeting, and digital delivery, and generated recommendations for NSSS 2026+. Together, these processes demonstrate that the CMC and its sub-structures are activated and overseeing significant reform progress, in line with the 2025 target.
The final NSSS Evaluation shows significant reform progress

The baseline budget was BDG 2,400 Crore, equivalent to approx. USD 205.13 million (at exchange rate 1 USD = 117 BDT). In 2025-26 fiscal year, the budget increased to BDT 4,791.31 Crore or approx. USD 409.51 million i.e. an increase of approx 99.6%.

title.narrative: Functional and utilized web-based social protection dashboard for improved monitoring of social protection reforms incorporated in the Annual Performance Agreement (APA)

description.narrative: SPPS will supprt GoB for the functional and utilized web-based social protection dashboard for improved monitoring of social protection reforms incorporated in the Annual Performance Agreement (APA)

comment.narrative: Government monitoring of social protection reforms is manual and does not cover all areas of reform performance.
The dashboard is finalized and populated with sex and age disaggregated data, including M&E Framework data
The dashboard is to be inaguaretd by the govt and populated with sex and age disaggregated data, including M&E Framework data,
Functioning dashboard with M&E sex and age disaggregated data developed for CMC sub-committee / M&E committee review, follow-up, and feedback to line ministries on social protection reform progress

Dashboard with M&E functioning developed for CMC sub-committee / M&E committee review, follow-up, and feedback to line ministries on social protection reform progress with ministries expected to share their sex and age disaggregated data.
NSSS 2026+ incorporates a section on the dashboard and revised M&E framework

The web-based NSSS Reform and Programme M&E Dashboard is fully operational to track Action Plan II implementation and to provide feedback to line ministries. Sex- and age-disaggregated fields are integrated. The draft NSSS 2026+ and its revised M&E framework explicitly reference the dashboard as a monitoring tool, and this approach was presented and endorsed in principle at the National Conference on Social Protection 2025.
NSSS 2026+ incorporates a section on the dashboard and revised M&E framework

title.narrative: Increase knowledge about reform priorities of 5 key line ministries (Cabinet Division, GED, MoSW, MoF, MoLE) with evidence of support from senior leadership to implement them

description.narrative: SPPS will support GOB to increase knowledge about reform priorities of 5 key line ministries (Cabinet Division, GED, MoSW, MoF, MoLE) with evidence of support from senior leadership to implement them

comment.narrative: Limited knowledge among key line ministries about key reform issues, specifically exclusion, social insurance, gender main responsiveness mainstreaming, and Persons with Disabilities
Senior officials of the line ministries have improved their understanding of global best practices on social protection models
Senior officials of the line ministries are using global best practices and models in social protection
Line ministries have provided high-quality inputs into key policy proposals

Line ministries have provided high-quality inputs into number of key policy proposals including draft NSIS policy, draft NSSS 2026+ with support from SPPS at a residential workshop with high-level officials with Cabinet Secretary as the chair.
Line ministries demonstrate significantly increased capacity to sustain the reform effort

In 2025, the five key line ministries (Cabinet Division, GED, MoSW, MoF, MoLE) moved from providing inputs to actively shaping and championing reform priorities. With SPPS support, line ministries contributed to the drafting process of NSSS+, the NSIS roadmap, and major policy processes such as the White Paper on the Economy, Task for Re-strategising the Economy, Women's Affairs Reform Commission, and Local govt. reform commission. Senior officials co-chaired and led sessions at the National Conference on Social Protection 2025, endorsed programme consolidation (from 140 to 95), expansion of OAA/PwD coverage, and stronger G2P delivery and data systems. This reflects stronger ownership, deeper understanding of reform, and visible leadership commitment to implementing an inclusive, lifecyle-based social protection system.
Line ministries demonstrate significantly increased capacity to sustain the reform effort

title.narrative: By 2025, Government has significantly increased coverage of old age persons and persons with disability by adopting an universal approach (sex and age disaggregated)

description.narrative: By 2025, Government has significantly increased coverage of old age persons and persons with disability by adopting an universal approach (sex and age disaggregated) as a result of collective advocacy along with the SPPS

comment.narrative:
Old age and Persons with Disabilities are not universal
OA coverage: 4.4m (2019)
Persons with Disabilities coverage: 1.8m (2019)

OA increased by 40% from baseline
Persons with Disabilities increased by 40% from the baseline
OA increased by 32% from baseline and Persons with Disabilities increased by 60% from the baseline

OA increased by 50% from baseline
Persons with Disabilities increased by 50% from the baseline

OA increased by 36% from baseline
Persons with Disabilities increased by 80% from the baseline

OA increased by 80% from baseline
Persons with Disabilities increased by 80% from the baseline

By 2025-26, govt. has substantially expanded lifecycle coverage. Old Age Allowance has increased significantly from baseline and is close to 80% coverage target, largely because G2P and NID-bassed verification continue to remove ghost duplicate beneficiaries even as new eligible beneficiaries are added. In contrast, the PwD allowance has surpassed the arget, the number of beneficiaries increased from 1.545 million at baseline to 3.531 million (an increase of 128.5%), clearly exceeding the 80% target an ddemonstrating strong progress towards universal, rights-based approach for persons with disabilities.

OA increased by 100% from baseline
Persons with Disabilities increased by 100% from the baseline

title.narrative: By 2025, Government has adopted gender and disability inclusive NSIS

description.narrative: SPPS will support GoB so that by 2025, Government has adopted gender and disability inclusive NSIS

comment.narrative: The NSIS feasibility assessment draft exists
The final draft NSIS policy is presented before the Cabinet Committee on Social Security (5 Ministers headed by the Minister of Finance)
The final draft NSIS policy is presented before the Cabinet Committee on Social Security
Final draft NSIS policy reviewed by the Cabinet chaired by Prime Minister

A consensus building dialogue was held in May. The Cabinet Secretary chaired the meeting. The two-day long in-depth workshop and a consultative dialogue was participated by eight key Secretaries and high officials of key ministries of the GoB. The policy level consensus is that the GoB will go for developing the NSIS in 2024 and will place to the Cabinet for discussion. A pension authority in the Finance Division so far has been introduced six pension schemes for different group of working population in Bangladesh. Out of six, five schemes are functioning, and the leftover last scheme will be in active from July 2025. The consensus building dialogue initially decided that the Pension Authority may work on the social insurance scheme, considering the Beveridge (GoB contributory role) and Bismarck (individual contribution) models.
Proposals for NSIS policy to is endorsed by the Govt.
Proposals for NSIS policy to is endorsed by the Govt.
Proposals for NSIS policy is place by the Govt.

title.narrative: By 2025,NSSS 2026+ adopted a genderand disability responsive universal social security approach

description.narrative: By 2025, NSSS 2026+ adopted a genderand disability responsive universal social security approach with the support form SPPS

comment.narrative: NSSS Mid-Term Review (MTR) available
CMC endorses NSSS 2026+ draft
A road Map 2026+ discussion paper presented in a policy consultative dialogue.
Final draft NSSS reviewed by the Cabinet chaired by Prime Minister

The Cabinet Secretary approved the proceeding of the dialogue in October 2022. The next generation NSSS 2026 Plus paper was presented in the DPs LCG forum, and the SSPS Programs PIC and PSC also discussed the issue of developing the next generation NSSS 2026 Plus.
A gender and disability-responsive NSSS 2026+ drafted

A gender- and disability-responsive NSSS 2026+ is under preparation. In 2025, a national NSSS consultation, joint field workshop with Cabinet Division and UNICEF, and the National Conference on Social Protection 2025 generated concrete recommendations on lifecycle, gender and disability inclusion, urban and adaptive SP. Gender diagnostics, Disability SP Framework and SWAPNO gender findings are feeding into the draft. Political transition and the need to complete the NSSS final evaluation have delayed completion and Cabinet submission beyond 2025.
A gender and disability-responsive NSSS 2026+ finalized

title.narrative: Ensured better management support to Project Board, Committees, programme components, budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting,

description.narrative: SPPS will supprot GoB to ensure better management support to Project Board, Committees, programme components, budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting,

comment.narrative: Lack of support for management support to Project Board, Committees, programme components, budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting on Social Protection
Ensured better management support to Project Board, Committees, programme components,
Management support to is provided to the Project Board, Committees, programme components.
Ensured better management support to budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting on Social Protection
Better management support ensured to budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting on Social Protection
Project Board, Committees, programme components is upported by project efforts

In 2025, the project continued to provide structured management and secretariat support to the Project Board (PIC/PSC), CMC and thematic committees, including preparation of concept notes, briefs, progress reports and minutes, and follow-up on agreed actions. SSPS supported planning and delivery of the National Conference on Social Protection, NSIS workshops, disability and gender events, and LCG/DP coordination meetings, ensuring alignment with the NSSS Action Plan. Budget management, audit follow-up, donor reporting (UNDP/DFAT), and quality assurance of key knowledge products were maintained, allowing the Board and committees to make evidence-based decisions on programme direction, reform prioritization and resource use.
Committees, programme components, budget, quality assurance, audits, partnership, DPs coordination, and reporting supported

title.narrative: PIMS-9850 Biodiversity Finance Plan Bangladesh

description.narrative: The initiative “Global Programme on National Biodiversity Finance Plans” is an enabling activity that directly supports the implementation of the new biodiversity targets under the new Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), specifically it will focus on providing support to countries in developing: a) a national biodiversity finance policy and institutional review; b) an expenditure review to assesses spending related to biodiversity across all relevant sectors; c) an assessment of financial needs required to achieve the targets of the GBF; and, d) a National Biodiversity Finance Plan. This is underpinned by a transformational process to develop a shared vision and action agenda on financing nature in the country, closely involving the finance sector.

title.narrative: IP-Sustainable Financing

description.narrative: UNDP Bangladesh will implement the Initiation Plan for Sustainable Financing from November 15, 2024, to 14 May 2026. The Sustainable Financing is an initiative for advancing UNDP Bangladesh’s work on sustainable financing across the key government agencies and non-government organizations to channel more financing for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in Bangladesh. This IP will be instrumental in updating and institutionalizing processes to integrate climate considerations into budgetary practices, strengthening government institutions to advance climate-responsive public financial management. Specifically, it will support the Finance Division within the Ministry of Finance in formulating a climate finance strategy, updating existing climate-budget tools and guidelines, building relevant capacities, and enhancing governance for climate-related public finance. At this stage, the primary focus of this IP is on engaging with the Ministry of Finance with the aim to promote inclusive budgeting and financing for climate resilience in Bangladesh

title.narrative: IP-Resilient, Sustainable and Inclusive Development (IP-RSID)

description.narrative: The UNDP Bangladesh will implement the Initiation Plan for Resilient, Sustainable, and Inclusive Development (IP-RSID) from July 1, 2024, to December 31, 2025. This plan aims to benefit the most vulnerable and marginalized populations in both rural and urban areas. Key outcomes include fostering a gender-responsive, cleaner, and more resilient environment, enhancing natural resources, promoting low-carbon development, and increasing resilience to climate change and disasters. The Initiation Plan will strengthen national capacity for managing climate-induced disaster risks through new programming opportunities. In addition, the IP commits to contribute to the Kigali amendment of reducing GHG and developing pathways for climate risk insurance, inclusive budgeting and financing for climate resilience, Islamic microfinance, and green Sukuk initiatives. Additionally, it will address solid waste management and strengthen disaster risk management and community resilience in Coxs Bazar, particularly for host and Rohingya communities. Innovations will be fostered through the UNDP Accelerator Lab, which focuses on exploring the blue economy, promoting sustainable tourism, enhancing the informal economy, and leveraging island development experiences. The combined efforts aim to establish an integrated program for creating healthier ecosystems.

title.narrative: IP-Transformative Economic Policy Programme (TEPP) -II

description.narrative: While graduation from Least Developed Country (LDC) status will be a testament to Bangladesh’s developmental progress, it will bring various challenges, requiring adroit policy responses to manage the transition and prepare, especially for the loss of LDC-specific trade preferences. If Bangladesh can successfully navigate and mitigate any adverse implications for its export trade following its graduation from the LDC status, the transition should not precipitate major economic consequences, but will rather represent a natural progression in economic development.The Ministry of Commerce (MoC) will play a crucial role in the post-LDC scenario, despite facing limited resources—encompassing human, financial, and technical capacities—and programmatic support. The Transformative Economic Policy Programme (TEPP) project, funded by the UK government, aims to bridge these gaps, aligning closely with national priorities for a smooth LDC graduation, particularly on Bangladesh’s trade policy and trade negotiation skills, inter-ministerial coordination on compliance issues, the private sector’s competitiveness, and evidence-based policy initiatives.Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) will also play a crucial role in supporting ongoing growth and economic development and reaping potential benefits from the LDC graduation. In an evolving context, Bangladesh will need to improve investment facilitation, retention, and aftercare services to attract more FDI. This project, through the Research and Analytics Unit (RAU), anchored in the Bangladesh Investment Development Authority (BIDA) of the Prime Minister’s Office will also support evidence-based policy reforms for improved investment climate in Bangladesh.

title.narrative: IP- Governance Portfolio_Integrated Support for Advancing Reforms and Democratic Governance in Bangladesh (SARG)

description.narrative: Following the establishment of an Interim Government in August 2024, this 18-month Initiation Plan (IP) aims to enhance the capabilities of interim authorities, national institutions, and civil society in Bangladesh to drive democratic reforms and foster inclusive development. It houses the early phase of UNDP support for democratic and institutional reforms in Bangladesh and the advancement and repositioning of ongoing governance programming. Central to this initiative is a commitment to restoring trust among stakeholders, engaging systematically with young people, civil society and citizens, and ensuring risk-informed and collaborative approaches. This IP will also serve as a foundational framework to consolidate and align UNDP’s democratic governance programming in Bangladesh, and for transitioning to a longer-term portfolio of strategic interventions thereafter.

title.narrative: BGD-TCCF Plastics Circularity

description.narrative: Plastics have become an integral part of daily life, used for items such as straws, shopping bags, electronics, and construction materials. The prevailing linear model of take, make, use, and dispose results in significant plastic waste accumulation, with forecasts suggesting 12 billion tons of plastic will be in landfills and the environment by 2050. Globally, the environmental, climate, and health impacts of plastics are nearing US$139 billion annually, adversely affecting freshwater systems, marine resources, terrestrial biodiversity, and public health. Adopting a circular economy would mitigate plastic production, use, and disposal.Urbanization and development have escalated plastic consumption and waste, often leading to clogging of rivers and water bodies. Plastics break down into harmful microplastics, posing risks to both humans and ecosystems. In Bangladesh, annual per capita plastic consumption in urban areas has significantly increased, with single-use plastic bags and multilayer plastics being major contributors to landfill waste. Addressing the challenges associated with plastics requires a life-cycle approach, including bans, recycling, and alternative materials. Nonetheless, without a comprehensive, multistakeholder plan grounded in circular economy principles, effectively tackling plastic issues remains challenging. Greater industry involvement, inter-ministry coordination, and attention to the informal sectors role are essential for creating a sustainable action plan.Implementing a market-driven decentralized circular economy model for plastic waste management in Chattogram, Cox’s Bazar, and Tangail aims to preserve the value of plastics in the economy while protecting the natural environment. This initiative includes policy advocacy and awareness efforts targeting government agencies, the private sector, city authorities, and local communities to foster a collaborative approach to waste management.

title.narrative: PIMS 6563 Community-based management of Tanguar Haor Wetland in Bangladesh

description.narrative: The project objective is to promote sustainable use of wetland resources by local communities to conserve globally significant biodiversity, improve ecosystem services and secure local livelihoods in Tanguar Haor. It is aimed at addressing the increased degradation of wetland habitat from unsustainable development and local community practices that is leading to biodiversity and ecosystem losses and as a consequence depletion of the productivity of the wetland. This is attributed to habitat degradation and fragmentation caused by expanding human settlement and agriculture, changes in hydrological regime caused by changes in land use patterns, pollution from discharge of effluents, dumping of waste, agro-chemicals and erosion of soil, over-exploitation and other unsustainable uses of natural resources, unplanned tourism, invasive alien species, particularly the introduction of exotic species of fishes and climate change impacts. The project is thus aimed at addressing these multiple threats by harmonizing socio-economic development, sustainable management of land and water resources and biodiversity conservation through an integrated ecosystem-based management approach in the wetland. To achieve this, actions will be taken to strengthen capacity and coordinated planning at the national and sub-national levels on socio-economic development as well as demonstrate sustainable fisheries, agriculture and natural resources management, biodiversity conservation and restoration, and alternative livelihood initiatives, including from a gender perspective on the other. This project intends to remove barriers towards establishing an integrated collaborative and participatory management approach effectively operational at all levels of conservation action including planning, implementation, monitoring and assessment to drive adaptive management processes to restore and improve the condition of the wetland.This will be achieved through the three inter-related and mutually com

title.narrative: PIMS-10174 GEF SGP 8th Operational Phase (Part 1) Bangladesh

description.narrative: SGP-OP8 builds on 30 years of successful experience in empowering local CSOs and CBOs in community driveninitiatives that have enhanced wellbeing, increased awareness and resilience, and generated globalenvironmental benefits. With economic development pressures intensifying in the developing world and theassociated rising inequalities, local communities, particularly vulnerable and disadvantaged groups are becomingincreasingly marginalized. The objective of Part 1 of SGP OP8 is to engage local CSOs/CBOs in landscape-seascapeapproaches across the 99 participating countries, providing them access to knowledge and information,capacitating them through learning-by-doing, skills development, and delivering technical and grant assistancefor interventions that enhance socioeconomic conditions and generate global environmental benefits. In line withDocusign Envelope ID: F68381B8-A2F6-4E7A-9B61-FD481C0E846C2 | P a g ethe GEF-8 SGP Implementation Arrangements Paper, core SGP resources are to be allocated equally among all eligible countries.The proposed strategy, elaborated through four complementary outcomes, reflects the key features of GEF Small Grants Programme 2.0, including new approaches to support youth, women and Indigenous Peoples, linking up with complementary partnerships, cooperating with the two additional GEF agencies, and leveraging opportunities with other GEF programs. SGP-OP8 will facilitate opportunities for innovation and scaling up, catalyze multi-stakeholder alliances, and leverage dialogue platforms for greater impact.An important comparative advantage of SGP is the focus on social inclusion and vulnerable groups, including women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, the elderly and youth. Expected results under OP8-Part 1 include 225,000 ha of land and ecosystems under restoration, 3,800,000 ha of landscapes under improved practices, 90,000 ha of marine protected areas under improved management, 270,000 ha of marine habitat unde

title.narrative: IP for Ecosystems Restoration and Re-silience for Development in the Chittagong Hill Tracts (ERRD-CHT)

description.narrative: This Initiation Plan aims to outline a comprehensive initiative for the Chittagong Hill Tracts (CHT). This programme coordinates three distinct yet interconnected projects, with separate funding sources, that collectively address key areas including empowering girls and women through inclusive access to education and skills, addressing climate change issues, ecosystem restoration, biodiversity conservation, livelihood improvement, supporting youth on climate change, nature-based solution, climate adaptive livelihood options, social cohesion, and strengthening inclusive and accountable governance in the CHT.

title.narrative: IP_Institutional Strengthening for Promoting Accelerated Transformation (ISPAT) -II

description.narrative: The Initiation Plan (ISPAT-II) aims to strengthen public institutions by promoting accountability and evidence-based policy reforms, enabling NGOAB to deliver NGO services more effectively, equitably, and inclusively. It ensures that civil society contributions to Bangladesh’s development agenda are better recognized, integrated, and scaled.In light of recent political developments and the Government’s renewed reform agenda—focused on digitalization, reducing the digital divide, and enhancing transparency—the IP will maintain continuity of UNDP’s ongoing ISPAT interventions while the multi-year DIM project is undergoing government approval.Once the DIM project is approved and operational, this IP will close and all resources and activities will transition to the DIM project. The IP is proposed for seven months, from 1 December 2025 to 30 June 2026, with the possibility of extension if the government approval process is not completed within this period.

title.narrative: Activity web page
Initiation Plan (IP) for Scaling the Local Government Initiative on Climate Change (LoGIC)

description.narrative: The Initiation Plan (IP) will run for a duration of 12 months; it will be a preparatory phase aimed at bridging the gap between the completion of LoGIC I and the development, approval, and launch of LoGIC II, which will lead to full-scale roll-out. The LoGIC IP is designed as a transition and readiness phase that directly supports the GoB in aligning climate adaptation financing and governance with national priorities and strategies. The goal is to provide support to the Local Government Division (LGD) to transition the LoGIC model to the national scale up and technical assistance to the Economic Relations Division (ERD) to initiate and complete technical studies (e.g. Cost-benefit analysis, feasibility studies, various tools and financing models etc.), capturing lessons learnt and setting the foundation for the operational, financial and institutional systems that are necessary for sustainable LoGIC II as a transformative, government-led/owned, and financially viable model for local climate adaptation. The Initiation Plan will also provide technical, operational, and policy support to the LGD and the ERD in preparing and funding the Development Project Proposal (DPP) and the Project Document for LoGIC II; designing a blended finance model; ensuring institutional memory and community engagement; conducting relevant feasibility studies on Nature-based Solutions (NbS), community resilience and partnerships with the private sector; developing schemes for linkages between value chains and vulnerable communities, strengthening institutional, legal and policy framework for the efficient service delivery etc. ensuring sustainability and long-term assistance. The IP phase will use the adaptive management approach so the changing political transition has minimum impact on the initiation of LoGIC II, with an option to ensure the project scale up with loans and grants as planned, or to utilize committed government’s funds which will be associated with the donor funds.

title.narrative: Initiation Plan for Cox’s Bazar IP-CXB Area-based Programming

description.narrative: This Initiation Plan establishes the analytical, strategic, and operational groundwork for the next phase of UNDP’s development engagement in Cox’s Bazar. It responds to the district’s evolving context, where drastically declining humanitarian assistance, persistent socio-economic vulnerabilities, environmental degradation, and heightened climate risks require a transition toward long-term, development-anchored sustainable solutions. The plan will enable the potential preparation of a comprehensive pro-gramme grounded in evidence generated by Cox’s Bazar Analysis and Reporting Unit’s (CARU) real-time analytics, disaster risk assessments, livelihoods and market studies, environmental diagnostics, and so-cial-cohesion research. The IP will facilitate stakeholder consultations, technical analyses, and design of scalable interventions across resilience, livelihoods, skills, waste management, digital inclusion, and governance, comple-menting UNDP’s national level programming. It will integrate conflict-sensitivity, gender equality, climate considerations, and social cohesion into the programme architecture. The expected output is a complete programme document with a results framework, risk and financing strategies, partnership modalities, and implementation arrangements. This IP will provide a solid foundation for mobilizing resources and scaling interventions that support both host communities and Forcibly Displaced Myanmar Nationals (FDMN) in a balanced and sustainable manner. This will position UNDP to lead a development-driven, government-aligned approach to strengthening resilience, inclusive economic opportunities, environ-mental sustainability, and social stability in Cox’s Bazar.

title.narrative: Activity web page
Transformative Economic Policy Programme (TEPP) - II

description.narrative: Bangladesh’s graduation from LDC status is a major milestone but brings challenges, including the loss of trade preferences and stronger export competition. Managing this transition will require effective policy responses, with the Ministry of Commerce playing a key role despite capacity constraints. Funded by the UK and implemented by UNDP Bangladesh, TEPP II supports a smooth post LDC transition by strengthening trade policy, negotiation capacity, inter ministerial coordination, and evidence based policymaking. The programme also supports improved foreign direct investment facilitation and reform efforts led by the Bangladesh Investment Development Authority. It places strong emphasis on gender responsive policies to protect women workers and entrepreneurs in trade exposed sectors. Given the country’s fluid political context, TEPP II adopts a flexible programming approach to adapt to change while advancing inclusive and sustainable economic outcomes.
